Welcome everybody. So good to see so many faces. Glad to have you all here. We are here for a Q&A with Dr. Chaldini himself. This is a Q&A for business questions, challenges you're having and as they pertain to the science of influence and the principles. If you have a question, please feel free to raise your hand by using the feature at the bottom, or you can type your questions into the chat area. First though, let's have Bob. Can you tell us a little bit of some new research you just have? Certainly. I'm glad that everyone's here. I want to welcome you. One of the things I try to do in these Q&A sessions is to add something new that isn't in the information that we've already presented. So we always get a head start on what everybody else who's not involved in our program doesn't get. I just read something yesterday, a new study that helps us understand how we can impact a serious societal issue that impacts in fact all of us, affects all of us. And it's the increased use of handheld phone conversations or interactions while people are driving. It produces distracted driving and the research shows that if you are on your phone in some kind of way, maybe searching for a phone number and dialing it, maybe even examining a text that you get a reply. If you are doing that for five seconds, it increases the likelihood of a crash by 900% for five seconds. This is an important issue, getting people to reduce the amount of time that they are on the phone while they are driving and interacting with their handheld. The other issue that affects us all is that as a result of all of the increase in handheld use while people are driving, our insurance premiums have gone up 30% just for that reason. So this is a big societal issue. How can the principles of influence affect it in a positive way, reduce that possibility. Well, there was a study done here in the United States with people who had signed up for an app that allowed the people who were managing the app to get telemetric information from them about how often they were using their phone. Whether they were driving while they were on their phone for how long they were driving while interacting on their phone and so on. And they did a study with all the people who were on this app for seven weeks. And of course there was a control condition who got no treatment. They were just left alone, right, to do what they normally did. Then there was a group that were given a payment for reducing the amount of usage of their handheld while driving. They were told that they could acquire up to $50 during the period of this test if they reduce their usage. Okay, that was one group. And what they did from that payment was reduce the number of seconds per hour that they were on the phone by 16 seconds. Now remember, for five seconds, you increase your likelihood of a crash by 900%. So they tripled that with just that payment. And as you all know, payment isn't really one of the principles of influence. That's something that we all understand it's not about persuasion in the way that I think about it. What I think about is persuasion in terms of the seven principles of influence. Well, they examined two more, two other drivers of change. One was to tell them how they were doing relative to their comparable other drivers who were in the test, people who were similar to them in age and sex and marital status and residence, whether they were in rural or urban environments and so on. And they gave them information about how they were doing at the end of every week of the seven weeks. That reduced seconds per hour to 24 fewer. All right, so pay them you get 16. Just tell them what their what their comparable others are doing their peers are doing and you get a 24% second reduction that's a 50% difference. Right, by using one of the principles. Okay, and then they decided, well, what if you combine those two. What if you paid them and gave them that information. Now, the reduction drops to 40 seconds, which is another huge difference. I mean, compared to the average of the other two just the mere average, that's a that's a 50% increase in impact by combining those two. Now we're in a position, we're not in a position usually to, to pay people to move in our direction. Right, so let's focus just on one other principle of influence that they could involve in this process. Well, a final group, they had payment plus social proof. And then they said, with regard to the payment, they use loss aversion. And they said, not you will be able to get up to $50, you would lose up to $50. And they get 56 fewer seconds per hour. And they did an analysis, they said, if this interest, this treatment could be scaled up nationwide, we would have one billion fewer seconds of of distracted driving just in the United States alone in one year, one billion. So we can really make a difference with these principles that we're talking about here, we're adding social proof to a payment thing that we really don't usually use paying people to move in our direction. We add social proof. And then if we add loss aversion if we add the scarcity principle, we get this dramatic influence. So the umbrella point here. And that is, we can really make a difference in our world with these principles. Second, you can stack the principles, you don't have to use just one. If there's more than one that is appropriate for this setting. We can put them together and compound the impact of what we do. Now I want to be cautious about this, because there's research to show that if you do to your fine you get these increase when you go to three or more, you start getting a reduction in the impact because people feel like they're being done into compliance they feel like they're being pushed and pressured. So, I would say to at most three. After that stop to use a another principle in a different communication with them, but not in the same one. So that's the latest new science that I can bring to bear on the kinds of issues that we face. And I'm happy to now address all questions or comments that people would have for the remainder of our, our time together. Sure, certainly, certainly Dr. Chaldeany that was very informative and I think one of the keys there is that there is a sweet spot, the just two to three principles and any more. And it can have the reversal effect. I'm feeling resentful of getting pushed. Yeah, go back. And now I'll tell you something that I wasn't going to mention but to your point. They had yet one more condition. They did everything the same. Except they doubled the payment to you could win as much as $100. And they reduced the impact. Because people were feeling like, wait a minute. Right. Now this is especially true for something like a good cause a public service related cause like reducing. I think with a commercial offer, we can up the the incentive and be fine. When you're dealing with something that's public service. People want to see themselves as doing it for their intrinsic moral and ethical and socially responsible reasons, not because they're being paid to do it. Yeah, such a such a powerful point there so without any delay here let's go to our raised hands Patrick did you want to go ahead and unmute yourself. Excuse me, I'm going to say one more thing before Patrick mentioned. I'm a shark in the water. When it comes to these findings, I am always prowling through the literature on behavioral science and social influence to find these kinds of things. I love it. And so I will always be doing it for you. Okay. Hello Dr. Jardini. Yeah, lovely to to talk with you again greetings from Melbourne to him. Hello. Congratulations also with the 40 year anniversary wasn't able to make it to America. We've kept up somewhere else soon. I've got two quick questions if I may. In relation to authority we know that we don't want to sing our own praises because people will conceive us as being boastful. How about when somebody does a presentation, let's say they have a slide behind them that lists their credentials would you say that that is acceptable is that like having certificates on the wall as a silent contributor or is that still taken as boastful. Better, but I would say optimal would be to have somebody introduce you in that presentation, or better to take those credentials and send them ahead to your audience in your resume or your credentials. I would say I'm looking forward to speaking with you on Thursday on the topic of X my credentials and background and experience relative as to X are as follows. So you're not in the same as personal interaction with people, you don't have to be a scene as boastful in that situation you lose credibility when you do that but if you send it ahead in a written document that is, you know your resume, your, your LinkedIn profile and so on. That's true. That's exactly the case you're not seeing seen as being boastful to them. You're seeing being you're seen as honestly enforcing or raising to consciousness, your genuine credibility. Very lovely. Thank you. And in relation to consistency, once somebody has taken an active or public step towards a certain issue. How can somebody. I mean, sometimes we come to the realization that we've chosen in the past was incorrect. How can we gracefully inform others that our focus has changed and we're taking a new new direction. I want to answer that question but first I, as you were mentioning it occurred to me that I've forgotten something else to recommend to your, your first question. And that is, if you are presenting something that includes a budget, or includes a estimate of the amount of money that you will be requiring, let's say, to do a job. Right. Always use, let me say it this way, don't use a round number. Don't say this, I'll be able to do this for you for $25,000. Say, I've done my recent and it's 25,112. This tells them that you have done your homework. It establishes your knowledge and your credibility and your, the extent to which you have put in the effort and gotten the exact right answer. And so that's one other way you can increase your authority without having to brag about yourself. You show your work. Okay, now, sorry to backtrack on that, but please ask that question again on consistency. So somebody has made an action or a statement in the past. And now they've come to the realization that well, you know what that was actually my values have changed. I want to take a different direction in relation to, to this action how can they gracefully in the eyes of others admits that well, yes, that's what I said in the past but I don't support that anymore I want to go in a different direction with that. Yeah, this is a good question and the answer would be, you don't say I just feel differently now because that makes you seem like just somebody who is blown around in the wind like a leaf, right but you say I've gotten new information, which has a cause me to go in the different direction, because that's what a good decision maker does takes into account, all the relevant information. And when you had that earlier position, you didn't have this information, you were still a good decision maker, using the information that was available. Now you've got some new information. And this caused you to have to rethink your position. So you still come off as a, as an elegant, optimal, optimal decision maker, as a result of someone who takes into account all of the relevant information, and that's the basis for the change. Wonderful. Thank you so much. Much appreciate it. Thank you so much, Patrick. Let's move on to Andre. Do you want to unmute yourself. Yes. Hello, Dr. Chalbini. Hello everybody. Greetings from Romania. So, I want to address a challenge that I got in training that I held a few days ago in a company. So usually for the trainings I use the lunch and the materials that we have on our platform. And in one of one of that slides we have that research from Daniel Kahneman. So that's saying that 95% of the decisions that we usually make day to day, it's made with system one, that rapid, uncautious system. So it's the most challenged with this question. So in a business meeting, when it's a negotiation, when maybe more, it's more rational thinking. Can we still sustain this claim? Can we say that this is the, this percentage, or it's maybe lower? No, in those meetings, that's the other 5%. But most of our lives, we aren't in meetings. We're walking down the street, we're driving our car and there's a stoplight. And there's, you know, we're in a crowd of people, we're at a store with signage regarding sales and so on. Those are the decisions that go on kind of mechanically, automatically, the ones that are over learned. We don't have to think about because they're so rehearsed in the past that we've come to be automatic in our responding there. But in those situations where we're asked to be very focused and cognitive and cerebral about the decision making process, that's when system two takes over. And I would say that the majority flips that. It's probably not 95%. But now most of the activity is done with system two kind of thinking with rationality and logic being important. That's not to say that the other principle, the other factors like liking, how much do I like this person? Scarcity, how much do I feel that I have my options are dwindling, social proof, those all work. But they work in terms of people factoring them into an equation that includes the logic of the merits of the situation. So still unconscious decisions in more rational decisions, the principles still apply. They still apply. Because if you think about them, they have their base in rationality. If the majority of people are doing this, it's rational for you to do it. If this unique opportunity, favorable opportunity is dwindling and availability, it's rational to seize it. If all the experts are, if the authorities are saying it's rational. So at their base, it's rational, but people don't go through the troll and con process. Once those principles are in place, right? They usually also influence us. But sometimes we stop and think, in addition, what are the pros and cons in terms of the financial expectations here? You do a business model kind of approach to things. Yeah, that becomes part of the decision making process that really isn't part of it when you're just in system one thinking. But system one and system two factors really do still apply in business negotiations. Okay. So, for example, there's a study that was done on business negotiators that if you start out by shaking hands with your business, your negotiation partner, you get a significantly better mutual outcome. Right. Now that's not rational, but that was what it what it signals is, oh, this is somebody who is willing to be cooperative to be mutually agreeable. Mm hmm. Now, so here's what I asked my, when I do talks to negotiators, and I present this, and they say, Oh, that's good. We don't always do that we should do that. I said, No, let me ask, are those people say, Yeah, we do do that. And I said, Okay, then when you break for lunch, and you come back, do you shake hands again? Say never. Wow. Why not? Why not? Why not really reinstate the power of cooperation, the salience at top of mind of a cooperative intent. Yeah. Okay, got it. Thank you very much. Thank you. That was a really good correct question. What, what you do. One thing is research shows that you get significantly more conversions to your online site. If on your landing page, there is a welcoming statement. If you say, welcome. We're so glad that you decided to join us here. You get significantly more conversions before people see anything about your offer. Because it's, you've humanized the interaction in a period of time when technology is separating us from one another. We're making for that human connection that is being drained away by technology that where we work separately from one another now, we do it to a screen. Right. We entertain ourselves separately we we punch Netflix or, or YouTube. We, we, we, we inform ourselves separately we go to, we go to wiki lakes, or we go to Google to find answers we're not interacting with one another. And as a consequence, people want more and more of the human connection. Like a welcome, like a genuine welcome. You know that the, the two categories of marketing that have shown the greatest increase in the last 15 years are our handmade products. And number one, because there's a person there. And to influencer marketing. There's a person there that you're interacting with this person, not some advertising agency and some commercial where you see something going on. Right. People are, are yearning for, for personal interaction. Here's another one. And this requires that we go a little further out than just business interactions, medical interactions. So there was a study done at a set of hospitals in which radiologists who were reading the scans the CT scans of patients and giving them evaluations and recommendations for use, got the scans of a particular individual. Just the scans, just the way they normally do, or they got the scans with a photograph of the patient. Added. They did three times more evaluation and recommendations if there was a person there. Just the photo of a person. On our websites. One of the first things we should do is have a picture of us or of our team. I Lee, who was introducing me helps me with my, my it and I asked her to change my email address so that you don't just see Robert Chaldeany at the bottom. You see a picture of me and a signature with my name. That's a person. I'm a, that's a person. Right. Those are the kind of things that we talk about how we can humanize what we're doing. One of the fun things I've seen Bob that people have done is on their about us page is they'll have their team. They'll have photographs of everyone and it'll be kind of a professional photo. But when you kind of swipe your mouse over, you'll get a funny photo of them, you know, something they enjoy doing in their spare time of or a hobby. And you get that kind of an explanation about them. So you really do feel more connected to them. I thought that was a very cool thing. I hadn't seen that. That's a great idea. Here's another thing I see that we recommend against and that is in those about us photos. Sometimes you get the professional photo stern. I'm ready for business. A person should be smiling. We want somebody who is giving us a welcoming appearance that they're smiling at us that they approve of us. Right. So, that's another thing I would recommend a smile among when you're, when you're doing this because the research shows that people are looking for there was a study done of. You go into a service company that be a physician or a dentist or exercise studio or a financial planner there's somebody behind the desk right there's somebody waiting. And people are asked what's the thing you most want from that person. When you approach, and it wasn't wisdom or experience, or even attention. It was a smile. They wanted a smile. What fools we would be of the influence process. If we didn't infuse the technology that separating us with features that bring us back together as humans. And that's a great point. It makes me think sometimes too when you're the one that gives the smile I don't know if you're on the subway or you're walking down the street or you're in the grocery store and you give the smile and you don't get one back sometimes. That's breaking the rule. That's right. And the rule of reciprocity applies and if you are, if you're passing somebody and you smile at them that significantly increases the likelihood of a smile in return. I wanted to piggyback on what you were talking about with insurance, because what I'll share has societal impact, but it also has personal impact for people. As the insurance industry transitioned into what's called telematics. And that's the technology that's in your car or you can download on your phone that monitors your driving. There was a lot of resistance to it. Because people would use ineffective words and insurance agent. Might talk about it tracks you and nobody wants to be tracked. Nobody wants big brother tracking them. And so I would always advise, you know, using a word like monitor, it'll monitor your driving. But the big thing was people are typically fearful of new technology. And so the question that I would always prompt them to ask was, do you consider yourself to be a good driver, at least better than average. Who's going to tell their insurance agent know, right. And that becomes that pre suites them and becomes a platform to step in and say, well, you know what, your insurance rates are based on averages, which means better than average drivers like yourself are overpaying. In fact, we find some are overpaying as much as 50%. Right. So now we've got scarcity in there. And then they would go in and say, you know, I've been doing this now for decades and I'm advising all of my clients to try it. And so now we get authority and we get consensus but the long and short of it is if you are a good driver, you will end up saving a significant amount. But the other thing it does too is it really gets you more focused on your driving. When you begin to get reports back, you start to self monitor because you don't want to see your rates go up. And so all of these factors together can can be beneficial for all of you on an individual level. And then when it's beneficial for all of you, it becomes societally much better as well. And what I always do is recommend when you introduce yourself you say I want, I'm very glad to be here and I want to answer any of the questions that you have for me. But before we do I wonder if you could answer a question of mine. So you take the initiative, right in your introduction of yourself to short circuit the possibility that they would. Okay, let's move on Philip hi how are you today Philip. Hi, I'm great. And very interested. Two questions. The first one is, is overwhelming social proof against climate action actually, because everybody around us is not really doing anything or not doing much. And I wonder how to how to get over this this immense proof against. Yeah, doing something to two ways, I think. The first is there, you've described the norm, what that we call the descriptive we've described what most people are doing. I'm not so sure that the great majority of people but say a lot of people are not acting in terms of friendly environmental responded. But the great majority of people are in favor of it, which is a different kind of norm, it's the, it's what's called the injunctive norm. It's the norm of what is approved by the majority of people. And you can point to statistics that show the great majority of people do approve it, and that has a separate impact. Then what people are doing versus the what they what they how they will evaluate you if they see you violating, you have a littering or doing something that that's against the norm. The other is if, and this is again something we do have available to us. If we don't have the majority. We can show trends. If you look at the trends of people who are taking environmental action over the last 20 years, there is a dramatic trend in that direction, and the trend, right, gets projected into the future. When we see a trajectory in a particular direction with at least three data points, you know, 10 years ago it was this five years ago it was this. This year it's this number of people who are recycling, let's say. That gets projected into the future where now we have something called future social proof in our direction, even if the majority are not the fact that there's an increasing minority is persuasive in itself. The second question is I work with people who say, I actually just want people to think for themselves. I don't really need this influence principles. And the problem is, they often work with people who don't consider arguments unless they really have to. So it's people who like are not in the mode of interacting with their arguments freely. And I have on the one hand to convince my clients to use influence principles to get to them, and I have to get them to give them the right influence principles to convince others to consider their actual arguments. Right. Well, I think what, fortunately, as we were talking about the principles of influence, don't require people to think through arguments. It's what most other people are doing. It's what the authorities are doing. It's what scares. It's what's consistent with what you've already done or said. It's those kinds of things we can provide to your clients as ways to move people without having to make them think hard in a cognitive way. Just give them the evidence and we can do it with, we can even do it with with imagery. It doesn't even have to be arguments that we make them work. So there was a study in the UK, where they were a shopping mall had a food center where you could go for lunch and there were various restaurants around the center all these big shop shopping centers have that. And people came between noon and one o'clock, and they so many that they didn't even have enough tables. So they were trying to convince people to have an early lunch. And they had posters that they presented of people. And one just said, have an early lunch. It's, you know, it's a good idea. One showed a person having an early lunch. Just an image of a person, this person is having an early lunch. And the other showed a group of people having an early lunch. And by far it was the one with the image of other people doing it that was enough to significantly increase the number of people who then came in the following weeks at an early time, because what what social proof does is it works for three reasons. One is it suggests Oh, this is the right thing to do. It's an adaptive thing to do it's smart to do this if a lot of people are doing it. Secondly, it's feasible. Oh yeah, these people could get off of work a little early and adjust their lunchtime. And finally, it's it's approved if if a lot of people are doing it it's probably something that that is approved you won't be an outlier you won't be seen as a deviant. So, so just an image like that would be enough that doesn't require having people walk through your arguments. Thank you. Dr. Thank you very much for the question Philip. And now Martin, John, how are you. How are we doing today? Thankfully, well, thank you, I really great to see everybody. So Dr. Chaldini's already answered this question rather well by email but maybe for the benefit of the group I'd like to ask it. This is about one of the lunch and learn materials we had on the study on consistency and commitment. When the actor goes to the beach and then goes to the ghost for a swim and there's a thief that steals the radio. I'm a little bit challenged from some of my delegates because I think the sample size in that in that study or at least what was my understanding is like 20 people. So my question is really so what's the kind of minimum threshold by which the studies can become exemplars of the principle. And then the case of the beach study how did that how did that become an exemplar. Right, this was an excellent question. And it has to do with the fact that for example, I, in my book influence, talk about the beach study where if people saw somebody at on the blanket next to them get up and walk down the beach, and somebody else came and stole a radio from their blanket. Only one out of 20 people intervene. But if the, if the individual on the blanket said could you watch my things, and they said yes I would. And so you get almost everybody jumping up and snatching the radio back or stopping that person and in some cases physically assaulting them. Right, because of commitment and consistency. Well, but Martin is right that's a study with 20 subjects per condition. And it's a great feature of the validity of that of that research. But why is it the one that is often cited. And what I said is that Martin had two points. What, which are the studies that get the most validation as worthy of showing an effect that we can be scientifically confident about. That's not the beach study. It's those studies that have large numbers of subjects with that have experimental randomized controlled conditions, and are scientifically rigorous. Okay. But those aren't the ones that exemplify the, the principle to a wide audience, who don't want to read about all that experimental rigor and analysis. They want one they can relate to one where they can see an effect working in a naturally occurring situation that they can put themselves in and say to themselves. Yeah, that's what I would do there. That's exactly what I would do. And that's the thing that you use as the exemplar provided and here's the big provision. That's important. Provided the results of that less than scientifically done study match the results of the scientifically done studies. Then you know that you're not steering any people, anybody in the wrong direction by using that as the one that you use as an exemplar for how this works. It does fit with the rigor. It's just in a situation where people can relate to it and recognize the power of it in a situation where they can put themselves in and recognize, oh yeah, this really works. Those are the two considerations that you have to take into account. Yeah, thanks. Thanks so much for your gracious reply and with sufficient scientific rigor as well. So thank you very much for that. Thank you very much. Karthik, you have a question again. I'll just briefly again, Dr. Sheldon, just one quick thing. I was just, this came across this news of some places having a little bit of civil unrest going on in their little localized in the cities, sometimes in the bigger context of a state and a little bit towards in the context of a country as well. So in such situations, how do leaders look at it as a through the lens of the influence principles and what are the best things that they can look at in that kind of a context? It's the principle of unity that you do have available to you that we are all members of the same culture. We're all members of the same nation. We're all Americans, we're all Brits, we're all French, whatever it is. And you use that in Spain, I see Polar is on our list, in Spain, there was a study that showed that if you describe to Spanish individuals in a survey and use the terms for the country as having to do with family, right, or having to do with a brotherhood, or a sisterhood, or having to do with unity and overall Spanish identity, those people become significantly more likely to fight and die for Spain, right. So to increase the sense that you do have available to you this. So for example, there was a study done in Israel with Palestinian and Israelis and they were both asked a series of questions. And, and then the researchers showed them the responses that they had that were similar to one another. They were, they all laughed at the same kind of joke. They all were disgusted by a government official who who used tax breaks for himself, but voted against them for a month. So, where you can get commonalities of shared values and shared priorities, you can raise the work, you know, we are, we're not so different. We are alike one another. And I'll tell you one other thing this I'm just kind of go off the, the, the topic of unity right now and go to social proof. There was a study done a series of studies done in Israel with Israelis and Palestinian, but Arab residents of Israel, right. And they were in a study where they were told we have a certain amount of money that we want you to distribute to various causes within Israel. And you have to negotiate. And there was a Palestinian and a Israeli negotiating with one another. And some of the possibilities for allocation were to have to do with security and the Israelis were, they wanted them, the money spent there. The Palestinians wanted it on public service education for minorities and so on. We want the money there. Okay. And they would negotiate and they were told if you can't come to an agreement on how to allocate this money. Nobody gets any money. And 65% of the time, nobody got any money because they were so separated from one another. They didn't trust the other person they would rather have their community lose money than have their rivals community gain money. Nobody won, unless the researchers said, you know, this is going to be a difficult negotiation. But in the past, several of our groups were able to find a middle ground and get a mutually acceptable allocation to both groups. And that flipped it. And now 65% of them did come to it. Just saying, by social proof, this is feasible. It might be difficult. It might be hard. Stay with it, because others like you were able to overcome this problem. And now you go from 65% walking away to 65% engaging in a mutually satisfactory allocation. Social proof, but it's the principle of it's the factor of feasibility that social proof provides. You can do it too. If your neighbors are saving energy, so can you. If other people are reducing their, like you are reducing the amount of time that they are on handheld phones. This is feasible for you to if the majority of a lot of people are having early lunches. This is feasible. It can be done. That's one of the strengths of the social proof principle. All right, thank you for that question, Karthik. We have time for one more question. Let's end it with our fantastic friend Pilar. How are you doing Pilar? Oh, you're muted. Here is it. Okay, sorry. I am in my iPhone. Actually, I was driving when you started. Well, maybe this question is a little bit more philosophical rather than practical, but I found myself that is easier. The more I know the principles to find out the way and the principles to apply in certain situations in the workplace. But then I'm struggling a little bit on my private life. I don't know because maybe because the people that really surrounds me, they know me really well, but mainly because I don't, maybe it has to be with my personality. I don't know. But the thing is, I'm struggling sometimes to use the principles on an ongoing basis rather than if I have to stop and think and then think how I'm going to approach the situation, then okay. But in an ongoing, then what I'm seeing is doing steeper things. I mean, I'm just jumping off all of the principles and it's like, oh my God, I did it again. So I just wanted to know, but if you have one recommendation, one tip that you can take to ask extravertists that we're really, you know, we're really impulsive. How to deal with this. And there's one that works for both sides in that with friends or family members and so on. And that is to raise the principle of unity before you begin and say, you know, we've been friends for a long time. We're members of the same family. Right. And that produces a sense of unity and a willingness to move in the right direction for both sides, because the we is the many means it's it's that's us those are those people are of us. And so we want them to benefit. They want us to benefit and we bring that to the top of consciousness at the outset as just a simple thing to do when it's true in our personal relationships that changes the way that we think about the interaction as in a in a positive way. Okay. And also break breaks. Yeah, breaks opens the space to, okay, we as a family that say I'm when you're saying that we're thinking on what you're going to say. Yeah, really makes sense. Thank you very much. All right. Thank you so much, everybody. Those were fantastic questions very thought provoking. They were very, very intelligent questions.
